 {"id":"bd-15c.8","title":"Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)","description":"# Implement evidence persistence for repair actions (JSONL)

## GOAL
Every repair action, corruption detection, policy decision, and scrub cycle must produce a durable, auditable evidence record persisted as append-only JSONL (JSON Lines).

## BACKGROUND
The alien artifact methodology requires an auditable evidence ledger for every decision the system makes. This is non-negotiable for FrankenFS's self-healing story. Without durable evidence, repairs are invisible black boxes — unacceptable for a filesystem.

## DESIGN

### Record Schema
Each JSONL line is a self-contained JSON object with:
- timestamp_ns: u64 — monotonic nanosecond timestamp
- event_type: enum { CorruptionDetected, RepairAttempted, RepairSucceeded, RepairFailed, ScrubCycleComplete, PolicyDecision, SymbolRefresh }
- block_group: u32 — affected block group number
- block_range: (u64, u64) — start/end block numbers
- corruption_details: { blocks_affected: u32, checksum_expected: [u8;32], checksum_found: [u8;32] }
- repair_details: { symbols_used: u32, symbols_available: u32, decode_time_us: u64, verify_pass: bool }
- policy_context: { corruption_posterior: f64, overhead_ratio: f64, risk_bound: f64 }
- outcome: enum { Success, InsufficientRedundancy, IoError, Timeout }

### Persistence Strategy
- Append-only JSONL file per filesystem instance
- File location: reserved metadata area or configurable path
- fsync after each critical record (repair success/failure)
- Batch fsync for scrub cycle records (performance)
- Rotation: new file per mount session, old files archived

### Integration Points
- ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs — scrub daemon emits ScrubCycleComplete records
- ffs-repair/src/codec.rs — repair pipeline emits RepairAttempted/Succeeded/Failed
- Bayesian autopilot — emits PolicyDecision records when adjusting overhead
- Mount/unmount — session boundary markers

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Every corruption detection produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every repair attempt (success or failure) produces a JSONL evidence record
- [ ] Every scrub cycle produces a summary JSONL record
- [ ] Records survive crash (fsync on critical events)
- [ ] ffs-cli evidence show can pretty-print the ledger
- [ ] Records are machine-parseable (valid JSON per line)
- [ ] Unit tests: serialize/deserialize round-trip for all event types","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"HazyCave","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:31.155123171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:50:33.672096188Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T17:50:33.671995168Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["evidence","jsonl","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.8","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:31.155123171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-15c.9","title":"Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss","description":"# Implement adaptive overhead ratio via Bayesian expected-loss

## GOAL
Replace ad-hoc threshold selection for RaptorQ overhead ratio with principled Bayesian decision theory. The system automatically adjusts repair symbol overhead (3-10%) based on observed corruption rate, using a formal expected-loss minimization framework.

## BACKGROUND
Current state: fixed overhead ratio. This is suboptimal — low-corruption drives waste space on repair symbols, while high-corruption drives may have insufficient redundancy. The alien artifact methodology demands principled, auditable policy decisions.

## MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK

### Beta Posterior over Corruption Probability
- Prior: Beta(alpha_0, beta_0) — weakly informative, e.g., Beta(1, 100)
- Observation model: each scrub cycle observes k corrupted blocks out of n checked
- Posterior: Beta(alpha_0 + k, beta_0 + n - k)
- Posterior mean: (alpha_0 + k) / (alpha_0 + beta_0 + n)

### Chernoff-Style Unrecoverable Risk Bound
- P(unrecoverable | overhead_ratio) = P(corrupted_blocks > decodable_symbols)
- For RaptorQ with overhead_ratio r and block group size B:
  - decodable_symbols = floor(r * B)
  - P(unrecoverable) = P(Binomial(B, p_corruption) > decodable_symbols)
- Compute via Beta-Binomial tail probability

### Expected-Loss Policy
- E[loss(overhead_ratio)] = P(unrecoverable) * data_loss_cost + overhead_ratio * storage_cost
- Minimize over overhead_ratio in [0.03, 0.10]
- data_loss_cost: configurable, default 1e6 (in arbitrary units)
- storage_cost: configurable, default 1.0 per unit overhead

### Metadata Elevation
- Superblock and GDT block groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- Rationale: metadata loss is catastrophic (entire FS unrecoverable vs single file loss)

### Update Schedule
- Recompute optimal overhead after each scrub cycle
- Apply lazily: next symbol refresh uses new ratio
- Log PolicyDecision evidence record with full posterior parameters

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
struct DurabilityAutopilot {
    alpha: f64,
    beta: f64,
    data_loss_cost: f64,
    storage_cost: f64,
    min_overhead: f64,  // 0.03
    max_overhead: f64,  // 0.10
    metadata_multiplier: f64,  // 2.0
}
```

### Key Functions
- update_posterior(corrupted: u64, checked: u64) — Bayesian update
- optimal_overhead() -> f64 — minimize expected loss
- optimal_overhead_metadata() -> f64 — with elevation multiplier
- risk_bound(overhead: f64) -> f64 — P(unrecoverable)
- expected_loss(overhead: f64) -> f64 — full loss computation

### Location
- New module: ffs-repair/src/autopilot.rs
- Integration: scrub daemon calls update_posterior after each cycle
- Integration: symbol refresh reads optimal_overhead before encoding

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Beta posterior correctly updated after each scrub cycle
- [ ] Expected-loss minimization selects overhead in [3%, 10%] range
- [ ] Metadata groups get 2x overhead multiplier
- [ ] PolicyDecision evidence records logged for every adjustment
- [ ] Unit tests: known corruption rates produce expected overhead selections
- [ ] Unit tests: edge cases (zero corruption, 100% corruption, single block group)
- [ ] Property test: optimal_overhead monotonically increases with corruption rate","notes":"## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::policy: fields checked_blocks, corrupted_blocks, groups_total on scrub-cycle observation intake.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::policy: fields posterior_alpha, posterior_beta, posterior_mean after posterior updates.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, metadata_group, selected_overhead, symbol_count, risk_bound, expected_loss for each policy decision.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, selected_symbols, layout_capacity, effective_overhead when clamping to reserved repair-block capacity.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::policy: fields group, error when policy evidence append fails.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderHare","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:53.168595969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T20:17:32.901024753Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T20:17:32.901001229Z","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive Bayesian overhead autopilot in ffs-repair with tests and full gate pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bayesian","policy","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:53.168595969Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:01.576524911Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":79,"issue_id":"bd-15c.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section.  {"id":"bd-16r","title":"Add stress test suite for concurrent MVCC operations","description":"# Add stress test suite for concurrent MVCC operations

## GOAL
Create stress tests that validate MVCC correctness under heavy concurrent load.

## BACKGROUND
MVCC is the heart of FrankenFS. Bugs here cause silent data corruption.
Stress tests must exercise:
- Concurrent readers with writers
- Multiple concurrent writers
- FCW conflict detection
- SSI write-skew detection
- GC under load
- Version chain growth/pruning

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Test Harness Structure
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod stress_tests {
    use asupersync::lab::Lab;
    
    #[test]
    fn stress_concurrent_rw() {
        let lab = Lab::new(SEED);
        lab.run(|| async {
            let store = MvccStore::new();
            
            // Spawn readers
            for _ in 0..100 {
                lab.spawn(reader_task(&store));
            }
            
            // Spawn writers
            for _ in 0..10 {
                lab.spawn(writer_task(&store));
            }
            
            lab.run_for(Duration::from_secs(60));
            
            verify_invariants(&store);
        });
    }
}
```

### 2. Invariants to Verify
1. **No lost writes**: Every committed write visible in correct snapshot
2. **Snapshot isolation**: Reader sees consistent state
3. **FCW correctness**: Conflicts detected, no silent overwrite
4. **SSI correctness**: Write-skew impossible
5. **GC safety**: No premature version pruning
6. **Memory bounded**: Version chains do not grow unbounded

### 3. Workload Patterns
- **Random**: Random blocks, random operations
- **Hotspot**: 90% ops on 10% of blocks
- **Sequential**: Sequential block access
- **Adversarial**: Maximize conflicts

### 4. Deterministic Replay
- Use Lab runtime for reproducible scheduling
- Seed-based RNG for deterministic operations
- Log operations for replay on failure

## TESTS
1. stress_concurrent_rw (1000 ops, 10 threads)
2. stress_fcw_conflicts (100% conflict rate)
3. stress_ssi_write_skew (classic scenarios)
4. stress_gc_under_load (continuous GC)
5. stress_version_chain_growth (max versions)

## LOGGING
- Operation log (trace): op, block, txn_id, result
- Invariant check (debug): what checked, result
- Failure (error): full state dump

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. No invariant violations in 1-hour run
2. Deterministic replay reproduces any failure
3. Memory usage bounded
4.  {"id":"bd-1mdk.2","title":"Implement Bw-Tree consolidation (delta chain compaction)","description":"# Implement Bw-Tree consolidation (delta chain compaction)

## GOAL
Implement background consolidation that merges accumulated delta records back into a base page, preventing unbounded delta chain growth and maintaining lookup performance.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard entry 8.7: Without consolidation, delta chains grow linearly with writes, degrading lookup from O(log n) to O(log n + chain_length). Consolidation creates a fresh base page incorporating all deltas, then atomically replaces the chain via CAS on the mapping table.

## DESIGN

### Consolidation Trigger
- Chain length threshold: consolidate when delta chain exceeds configurable max (default: 16 deltas)
- Background thread: periodic scan of all pages for chains exceeding threshold
- Opportunistic: reader that encounters long chain can trigger inline consolidation

### Consolidation Protocol
1. Pin epoch (prevent GC of chain during consolidation)
2. Traverse delta chain, collecting all Insert/Delete operations
3. Apply operations to base page, producing new consolidated base
4. CAS mapping_table[page_id] from old chain head to new base
5. On CAS success: defer_destroy old chain nodes
6. On CAS failure: discard new base, retry (new deltas appended concurrently)

### Split/Merge During Consolidation
- If consolidated page exceeds max size: split into two pages
- If consolidated page below min size and sibling below threshold: merge
- Split/Merge are themselves delta records, enabling concurrent structural modifications

### Structural Modification Protocol (SMO)
- Split: create new sibling page, append Split delta to original, append Insert delta to parent
- Merge: append Merge delta to removed page, append Delete delta to parent
- All via CAS — no locks, no latches

## RUST IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
- Consolidation can race with concurrent inserts: CAS handles this correctly
- crossbeam-epoch Guard must be held during entire consolidation
- New base page: Owned::new(PageDelta::Base(consolidated_node))
- Location: ffs-bwtree/src/consolidation.rs

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Consolidation merges delta chain into fresh base page
- [ ] CAS atomically replaces old chain with consolidated base
- [ ] Concurrent writers during consolidation handled (CAS retry)
- [ ] Chain length bounded by consolidation threshold
- [ ] Split triggered when consolidated page too large
- [ ] Merge triggered when consolidated page too small
- [ ] Unit tests: consolidation produces correct base page
- [ ] Unit tests: concurrent consolidation + insert race
- [ ] Benchmark: lookup latency before/after consolidation

 {"id":"bd-1mdk.3","title":"Add comprehensive unit tests for Bw-Tree operations","description":"# Add comprehensive unit tests for Bw-Tree operations

## GOAL
Build a thorough unit test suite verifying Bw-Tree correctness: single-threaded CRUD, concurrent operations, consolidation correctness, split/merge structural modifications, and memory safety (Miri-compatible).

## TEST CATEGORIES

### 1. Single-Threaded CRUD
- Insert 1000 keys, verify all lookups return correct values
- Delete half the keys, verify deleted keys return None
- Insert duplicate keys (update semantics), verify latest value wins
- Range scan: iterate keys in sorted order, verify ordering

### 2. Concurrent Insert/Lookup
- 4 threads each inserting 1000 unique keys concurrently
- After all threads complete: verify all 4000 keys present
- During inserts: 4 reader threads continuously looking up random keys
- Readers must never see partial/corrupt state

### 3. Concurrent Insert/Delete
- 2 writer threads: one inserting, one deleting (non-overlapping ranges)
- After completion: inserted range present, deleted range absent
- Overlap test: both threads target same keys, verify no lost updates

### 4. Consolidation Correctness
- Insert 100 deltas on one page, trigger consolidation
- Verify consolidated base page contains correct merged state
- Insert during consolidation: verify new delta visible after consolidation

### 5. Split/Merge
- Insert enough keys to trigger page split
- Verify both pages contain correct key ranges
- Delete keys to trigger merge, verify single page remains
- Concurrent split: multiple threads triggering splits on adjacent pages

### 6. Memory Safety (Miri)
- Small-scale tests (100 keys) runnable under Miri
- Verify no use-after-free, no data races, no undefined behavior
- crossbeam-epoch reclamation: verify old nodes freed after epoch advance

### 7. Edge Cases
- Empty tree: lookup, delete, range scan on empty tree
- Single-element tree: all operations
- Maximum chain length: verify consolidation triggers at threshold
- CAS retry exhaustion: verify graceful handling (if implemented)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 7 test categories implemented and passing
- [ ] Concurrent tests use deterministic seeding for reproducibility
- [ ] Miri tests pass (no UB detected)
- [ ] Test coverage: >= 80% line coverage for bwtree module
- [ ] Each test has descriptive name and documents expected behavior
- [ ] Logging: test harness captures tracing output for debugging failures","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralReef","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:35.295345664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T00:11:05.317085042Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T00:11:05.317042362Z","close_reason":"Added 16 comprehensive Bw-Tree unit tests covering: single-threaded CRUD (1000 keys insert/delete/lookup), duplicate insert update semantics, sorted materialization, concurrent insert+lookup (4+4 threads), concurrent insert+delete (non-overlapping and overlapping), empty page ops, single-element ops, unallocated page error, page allocation exhaustion, consolidation of empty page, threshold-based scan and selective consolidate_all, multi-page independence.  {"id":"bd-1mdk.4","title":"E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput","description":"# E2E benchmark: Bw-Tree vs locked B-tree extent index throughput

## GOAL
Prove that the Bw-Tree extent index provides superior concurrent throughput compared to the current locked B-tree implementation. This is the justification benchmark for the entire Bw-Tree epic.

## BENCHMARK SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Read-Heavy (95% lookup, 5% insert)
- Simulate metadata-heavy workload (readdir, stat)
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: lookups/sec, p50/p95/p99 lookup latency
- Expected: Bw-Tree scales linearly (readers never block); locked B-tree saturates

### Scenario 2: Write-Heavy (30% lookup, 70% insert/delete)
- Simulate file creation/deletion workload
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: operations/sec, CAS retry rate, consolidation frequency
- Expected: Bw-Tree outperforms at >= 4 threads (CAS contention < lock contention)

### Scenario 3: Mixed Workload (realistic filesystem)
- 50% lookup, 30% insert, 10% delete, 10% range scan
- Thread counts: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16
- Measure: aggregate ops/sec, per-operation latency breakdown
- Expected: Bw-Tree advantage increases with thread count

### Scenario 4: Single-Threaded Baseline
- Pure sequential operations (no concurrency)
- Measure: Bw-Tree overhead vs B-tree (delta chain traversal cost)
- Expected: B-tree faster single-threaded (simpler structure); Bw-Tree within 2x

### Scenario 5: Consolidation Overhead
- Measure: consolidation frequency, duration, and impact on concurrent operations
- Stress test: maximum write rate with consolidation enabled/disabled
- Expected: consolidation adds < 10% overhead at steady state

## METRICS
- Operations per second (throughput)
- Latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per operation type
- CAS retry rate (contention measure)
- Consolidation frequency and duration
- Memory usage (peak RSS)
- Scaling efficiency: ops/sec(N threads) / ops/sec(1 thread)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Bw-Tree outperforms locked B-tree at >= 4 concurrent threads for read-heavy workload
- [ ] Bw-Tree single-threaded overhead is within 2x of locked B-tree
- [ ] CAS retry rate < 10% under realistic workloads
- [ ] Consolidation overhead < 10% at steady state
- [ ] Results stored as JSON CI artifacts for regression tracking
- [ ] Benchmark harness is deterministic and reproducible
- [ ] Logging: tracing output captures per-scenario timing and contention metrics","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralReef","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:51.899776011Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T00:19:49.752844954Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T00:19:49.752760887Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmark","bwtree","e2e"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:51.899776011Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1mdk.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:21:51.899776011Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":162,"issue_id":"bd-1mdk.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-1wtx","title":"Implement Lab-based deterministic concurrency tests","description":"# Implement Lab-based deterministic concurrency tests

## GOAL
Create deterministic tests for all concurrency-sensitive code using asupersync Lab runtime.

## BACKGROUND
From AGENTS.md: "Deterministic testability. Concurrency-sensitive logic must be testable under asupersync lab runtime."

Lab provides:
- Seed-controlled scheduling
- Reproducible interleaving enumeration
- Deterministic failure injection

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Lab Test Infrastructure
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod lab_tests {
    use asupersync::lab::{Lab, LabConfig};
    
    fn run_lab_test<F, Fut>(name: &str, seeds: &[u64], f: F)
    where
        F: Fn(Lab) -> Fut,
        Fut: Future<Output = ()>,
    {
        for &seed in seeds {
            let config = LabConfig::new().seed(seed);
            let lab = Lab::new(config);
            lab.block_on(f(lab.clone()));
        }
    }
}
```

### 2. MVCC Lab Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn lab_fcw_conflict_detection() {
    run_lab_test("fcw_conflict", &[0, 42, 12345], |lab| async {
        let store = MvccStore::new();
        
        let t1 = store.begin();
        let t2 = store.begin();
        
        lab.spawn(async { t1.write(block, data).await });
        lab.spawn(async { t2.write(block, data).await });
        
        let results = lab.join_all().await;
        // Exactly one should fail with conflict
        assert\!(results.iter().filter(|r| r.is_err()).count() == 1);
    });
}
```

### 3. Cache Lab Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn lab_arc_concurrent_access() {
    run_lab_test("arc_concurrent", &[0, 1, 2], |lab| async {
        let cache = ArcCache::new(100);
        
        for i in 0..10 {
            lab.spawn(async move {
                for j in 0..100 {
                    cache.get_or_insert(block(j), fetch_block).await;
                }
            });
        }
        
        lab.join_all().await;
        cache.verify_invariants();
    });
}
```

### 4. Failure Injection
```rust
lab.inject_failure("disk_read", 0.01); // 1% failure rate
lab.inject_delay("commit", Duration::from_micros(100));
```

## TESTS
1. lab_fcw_conflict_detection (multiple seeds)
2. lab_ssi_write_skew (multiple seeds)
3. lab_gc_concurrent_access (multiple seeds)
4. lab_cache_eviction_race (multiple seeds)
5. lab_failure_injection (verify recovery)

## LOGGING
- Lab seed (info): for reproduction
- Schedule trace (trace): for debugging
- Invariant violation (error): full state dump

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All concurrency-sensitive code has Lab tests
2. Tests pass with 1000 different seeds
3. Failures reproducible with seed
4.  {"id":"bd-32yn.6","title":"Conformance: fsx-style stress test with corruption injection","description":"# Conformance: fsx-style stress test with corruption injection

## GOAL
Implement an fsx-style (File System eXerciser) stress test that performs random file operations while continuously verifying data integrity. Extended with FrankenFS-specific corruption injection to test self-healing under stress.

## BACKGROUND
fsx (from LTP/xfstests) is the gold standard for filesystem stress testing. It performs random reads, writes, truncates, and mmaps on a file, maintaining an in-memory reference copy. After each operation, it verifies the file matches the reference. Any mismatch is a bug.

## DESIGN

### Operations (weighted random selection)
- write(offset, length, data): 40%
- read(offset, length) + verify: 25%
- truncate(length): 10%
- fsync(): 10%
- fallocate(offset, length): 5%
- punch_hole(offset, length): 5%
- close + reopen: 5%

### Verification
- In-memory reference buffer tracks expected file content
- After each read: compare against reference
- Periodically: close file, reopen, read entire file, compare against reference
- After unmount + remount: verify entire file against reference

### Corruption Injection Extension
- Every N operations (configurable, default 1000): inject random block corruption
- Wait for scrub daemon to detect and repair
- Verify file still matches reference after repair
- Evidence ledger check: repair logged correctly

### Parameters
- File size: configurable (default 64MB)
- Operation count: configurable (default 100000)
- Seed: deterministic
- Duration mode: run for N seconds instead of N operations

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Zero data mismatches across 100K operations
- [ ] Survives corruption injection + repair cycles

 {"id":"bd-32yn.7","title":"E2E test: xfstests generic and ext4 subsets","description":"# E2E test: xfstests generic and ext4 subsets

## GOAL
Run the standard Linux filesystem test suite (xfstests/fstests) against FrankenFS FUSE mount to verify compatibility with expected filesystem semantics.

## BACKGROUND
xfstests (github.com/kdave/xfstests) is the authoritative filesystem test suite used by all major Linux filesystems. It contains:
- generic/: tests applicable to all POSIX filesystems (~600 tests)
- ext4/: tests specific to ext4 semantics (~100 tests)

## APPROACH

### Phase 1: Generic Subset
Run xfstests generic/ tests that apply to FUSE filesystems. Exclude tests requiring:
- Direct I/O (O_DIRECT) — FUSE may not support
- Filesystem-specific ioctls
- Quota management
- ACLs (unless we implement them)
- Kernel-specific features (fiemap, etc.)

Expected pass rate: start with identifying which tests pass, create tracking for regressions.

### Phase 2: ext4 Subset
Run xfstests ext4/ tests. Many require mkfs.ext4 features we may not support yet.
Subset: tests covering basic file operations, directory operations, extent handling.

### Infrastructure
- xfstests requires: TEST_DEV (FrankenFS FUSE mount), SCRATCH_DEV (second mount for temp data)
- Wrapper script: configure xfstests for FrankenFS, run selected tests, capture results
- CI integration: run on every PR, track pass/fail count over time

### Known Exclusions
- Tests requiring root (CAP_SYS_ADMIN)
- Tests requiring kernel features not available in FUSE
- Tests for features we explicitly don't implement (quotas, ACLs, etc.)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] xfstests infrastructure configured for FrankenFS
- [ ] Generic test subset identified and documented
- [ ] ext4 test subset identified and documented
- [ ] CI integration: test results tracked per commit
- [ ] Pass rate monotonically increases over time
- [ ] Regression: any previously passing test that fails blocks merge","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SnowyMeadow","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:29:22.445755731Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T21:31:27.906718473Z","closed_at":"2026-02-16T21:31:27.906686864Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","e2e","xfstests"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32yn.7","depends_on_id":"bd-32yn","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:29:22.445755731Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":124,"issue_id":"bd-32yn.7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

## GOAL
Provide unit test coverage for the Testing and Fault Injection epic (bd-32yn). The epic has many E2E/integration test beads (bd-32yn.1 through bd-32yn.7) but NO unit tests for the fault injection framework itself -- the pluggable I/O layer, failure point registration, deterministic replay, and crash-point scheduling.

## TEST PLAN

### Pluggable I/O Layer Tests
1. FaultInjector wraps BlockDevice transparently (no faults = passthrough)
2. fail_at_byte(offset) triggers fault at exact byte
3. fail_on_read(block) returns injected error
4. fail_on_write(block) returns injected error
5. Fault cleared after trigger (one-shot mode)
6. Persistent fault mode (fault on every access)

### Deterministic Replay Tests
7. Same seed produces same fault sequence
8. Fault sequence is reproducible across runs
9. Fault log captures all injected faults with timestamps

### Crash Point Tests
10. Crash point registered at specific operation
11. Crash point fires at correct moment (before/after fsync)
12. Recovery after crash point: filesystem mounts cleanly

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
All 12 tests above. Deterministic.

## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- Fault injected (debug): fault_type, target_block, operation
- Crash point fired (info): crash_point_id, operation_in_flight

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. All 12 tests pass
2. Framework is reusable across all fault injection beads
3.  {"id":"bd-375","title":"EPIC: btrfs Read Path & FUSE Mount","description":"# EPIC: btrfs Read Path & FUSE Mount

## PURPOSE
Enable read-only btrfs FUSE mounting, achieving parity with ext4 read-only support. Currently btrfs metadata parsing is at 35% (7/20).

## BACKGROUND
From FEATURE_PARITY.md, btrfs gaps:
- btrfs transaction parity: ❌ NOT IMPLEMENTED
- btrfs delayed refs parity: ❌ NOT IMPLEMENTED
- btrfs scrub parity: ❌ NOT IMPLEMENTED
- FUSE mount for btrfs: NOT WORKING

However, significant foundation exists:
- Superblock decode: ✅
- B-tree header decode: ✅
- Leaf item metadata decode: ✅
- Internal node parsing: ✅
- sys_chunk mapping: ✅
- Read-only tree walk: ✅
- BtrfsContext in ffs-core: ✅

The remaining gap is wiring tree walking and extent resolution into `FsOps` and then into the FUSE surface.

## CURRENT STATE
- `ffs-ondisk` has comprehensive btrfs parsing
- `ffs-btrfs` provides `walk_tree`
- `ffs-core` has a `BtrfsContext` stub
- `ffs-fuse` operations currently route to ext4-only `FsOps`

## GAPS TO CLOSE
1. Implement `BtrfsFsOps` (like `Ext4FsOps` but for btrfs)
2. Wire `ffs-cli mount` path to btrfs ops
3. Implement btrfs inode -> extent resolution sufficient for read-only file reads
4. Add E2E coverage for btrfs mount

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. `ffs mount btrfs.img /mnt` works read-only
2. Basic file operations work: `ls`, `cat`, `stat`
3. Subvolume visibility (at least default subvol)
4. Performance baseline established (hyperfine) for inspect + mount smoke
5. btrfs metadata parsing reaches 60%+ parity

## DEPENDENCIES
- EPIC: Conformance & Quality Infrastructure (bd-2jk) - need fixtures/goldens
- btrfs golden fixtures (bd-2jk.2)

## EXPLICIT V1 EXCLUSIONS (per PLAN_TO_PORT_FRANKENFS_TO_RUST.md)
- Multi-device / RAID: OUT OF SCOPE
- Compression: OUT OF SCOPE
- Send/receive: OUT OF SCOPE
- Write support: OUT OF SCOPE

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- EXISTING_EXT4_BTRFS_STRUCTURE.md §4-6 (btrfs behavior)
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §2 (btrfs format)

## Success Criteria
1. `ffs inspect <btrfs.img>` and `ffs mount <btrfs.img>` work on the golden fixtures without panics.
2. `scripts/e2e/ffs_btrfs_ro_smoke.sh` passes (or skips cleanly when FUSE unavailable) and produces actionable logs.
3. Unit tests cover:
   - inode lookup by key
   - directory enumeration ordering/offsets
   - extent resolution for file reads","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-12T14:56:58.365480057Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T23:05:32.043983776Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T23:05:32.043968297Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["btrfs","core","fuse"],"comments":[{"id":144,"issue_id":"bd-375","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization.
